title: Electricity
subject: physics
summary: Electricity is a high-value SPTG topic because many questions are formula-based but still contain conceptual traps.
The main focus is current, voltage, resistance, Ohm’s law, simple circuits, electrical power, and energy usage.
Harder questions often involve series-parallel combinations, current division, and choosing the correct power formula.
coreConcepts: Electric current is the rate of flow of charge.
Voltage is the electrical potential difference that drives current.
Ohm’s law connects voltage, current, and resistance through V = IR.
In a series circuit, current is the same through all components.
In a parallel circuit, voltage is the same across each branch.
Equivalent resistance in series adds directly.
Equivalent resistance in parallel decreases and is found by reciprocal addition.
Electrical power is the rate at which electrical energy is transferred.
Electrical energy used depends on power and time.
derive: Current is defined as charge per unit time, so I = Q/t.
Ohm’s law gives V = IR for ohmic conductors.
In a series circuit, total voltage is the sum of voltage drops, so equivalent resistance adds: Req = R1 + R2 + ...
In a parallel circuit, branch currents add while voltage stays the same, leading to 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ...
Power is energy per unit time, so P = W/t.
Since W = VQ and I = Q/t, electrical power becomes P = VI.
Substituting V = IR gives P = I^2R and P = V^2/R.
traps: Do not use series rules in parallel circuits or vice versa.
In series, current is the same; in parallel, voltage is the same.
Equivalent resistance in parallel is always less than the smallest branch resistance.
Power formulas are equivalent, but only use the one consistent with known values.
A larger resistance does not always mean larger total current effect; context matters.
Be careful with units: hours and seconds give different energy values.
If voltage is fixed, increasing resistance reduces current.
If current is fixed, increasing resistance increases voltage drop.

sptgSet.question: A current of 2 A flows for 5 s. Find the charge transferred.
A resistor of 4 Ω carries a current of 3 A. Find the voltage across it.
A bulb operates at 24 V and draws 2 A. Find its resistance.
Two resistors 3 Ω and 5 Ω are connected in series. Find the equivalent resistance.
Two resistors 6 Ω and 3 Ω are connected in parallel. Find the equivalent resistance.
A device uses 60 W for 10 s. Find the energy used.
A 12 V battery is connected to a 4 Ω resistor. Find the current.
A resistor has current 2 A and voltage 10 V. Find the power.
A 5 Ω resistor carries current 4 A. Find the power dissipated.
An appliance rated 1000 W runs for 2 hours. Find the energy used in kWh.

hardSet.question: Three resistors 2 Ω, 3 Ω, and 5 Ω are connected in series to a 20 V supply. Find the circuit current.
Two resistors 4 Ω and 12 Ω are connected in parallel to a 12 V source. Find total current.
A 24 V source is connected to two series resistors 4 Ω and 8 Ω. Find the voltage across the 8 Ω resistor.
A 12 V battery supplies a 6 Ω resistor and a 3 Ω resistor in parallel. Find the current through the 6 Ω branch.
A 12 V battery supplies a 6 Ω resistor and a 3 Ω resistor in parallel. Find the total current.
A heater rated 2000 W runs for 30 minutes. Find energy used in joules.
A resistor uses 50 W at 10 V. Find its resistance.
A resistor of 8 Ω dissipates 72 W. Find the current through it.
A battery delivers 120 C of charge in 1 minute. Find the current.
A lamp uses 0.5 kWh in one day. Find its average power in watts.

pat3Set.question: A 24 V source is connected to a 4 Ω resistor in series with a parallel combination of 6 Ω and 3 Ω. Find the total current.
For the same circuit, find the voltage across the parallel section.
For the same circuit, find the current through the 6 Ω branch.
Three resistors 2 Ω, 2 Ω, and 4 Ω are connected with the two 2 Ω resistors in parallel, then in series with 4 Ω. Find equivalent resistance.
A resistor network has 3 Ω and 6 Ω in parallel, connected in series with 2 Ω, across 12 V. Find total power.
A 10 Ω resistor is connected to 20 V for 5 minutes. Find total heat energy produced.
A device operates at 90% efficiency and outputs 180 W. Find input power.
A 12 V battery is connected to a variable resistor. If current doubles when resistance is halved, explain using Ohm’s law.
pat3Set.answer: Since I = V/R and V is constant, halving R doubles I

pat3Set.question: A 60 W, 240 V lamp is used for 10 hours. Find energy consumed in kWh and in joules.
A resistor network has total resistance 6 Ω on a 12 V source. Find current and total power.
